chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Next we are going to talk about how do we create NumPy arrays plus we will also discuss a lot of different properties

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: which are associated with NumPy arrays.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: To create NumPy arrays, let us remove these cells.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: I will also give you the entire code and the short notes for this entire lecture.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So you don't need to worry about me deleting anything.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We are actually going to get everything.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: But it is important that we also code together so that we are also learning things.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So the easiest way to create NumPy arrays is to create them from lists.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Python has lists and we can directly create them from them.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: For example, we can write np.array and we can pass any list here.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: This is a one-dimensional list, a flat list.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So we can create our NumPy array from this.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We can print the array and we can also print the type of the array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So we have created an array whose type is numpy.ndarray.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Similarly, if we want, we can create a separate array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Let's call it array2.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We can also print array2 and what we will do in it is we will add a string in it.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So let's run the code.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So now we have an array of strings.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We talked about NumPy arrays being homogeneous.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: If we send different types of data in the form of a list,

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: then NumPy will try to convert all the data into a single data type.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So that is why it is all converted into a single data type.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: To print the data type, we have a special property which is called array.dtype.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We can print the data type from array.dtype and here for this particular array, the data type is U21.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Now this is a little strange data type.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We will feel that what is the meaning of U here?

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: U means that strings are stored in Unicode format in our Numpy array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Here also, if we want, we can print the data type of the array and this is going to be int64.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Meaning integer values are stored in this array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Plus we also have one more property which is the shape property.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Shape means dimensions.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We can print the shape of these arrays here.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Now here because both of these arrays are one dimensional,

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: one dimensional array means a single list like a normal list.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So in 1D array, the dimension means the total number of elements.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So here we have 5 elements, so the dimension is going to be 5.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Here we have 6 elements, so the dimension is going to be 6.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Along with this, if we want, we can also create 2D arrays from 2D list.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: What does 2D list mean?

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Let's suppose we have some matrix type data like this.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We have some rows and we also have some columns.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So in this data, if we have data like this,

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: when we talk horizontally, we say that these are our rows.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: And when we talk vertically, we say that these are our columns.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: And we can represent this type in the form of 2D list.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Let's suppose we have data in our list.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We will create this data in 2D list first and then convert it in 2D array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So first of all, for 2D list, we actually store the data in terms of all the rows.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So in our 2D list, first row will come, second row will come, then third row will come.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So in our 2D list, we will have data 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: And we will convert this data in NumPy array to create a 2D array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: We can give it a name here, array2D.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: And we can print the array 2D.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So our 2D array will be created in this way.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: The same type of matrix has been created which we visualized here.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Plus we can also print the shape of this 2D array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Now here we will get a very interesting shape which is 3 x 3.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: 3 x 3 is basically the array dimension.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Whenever we talk about NumPy arrays, when we are printing the shape,

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: shape basically stands for dimension.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Dimension means what is the m x n value.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: What is the row multiplied by column value.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Now here we are not multiplying both of them, but we separate them from comma

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: that how many rows and columns we have.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: In this 2D array, we have 3 rows and 3 columns.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: That is why the shape is 3 x 3 or 3, 3.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: If we add one more row in it, 10, 11, and 12.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Then our dimension will be 4 x 3.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: That means we have 4 rows and 3 columns.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Our dimension should be a proper rectangular dimension.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: It is not that we can add numbers randomly.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: It is not that I can add a 13 after 12.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Because this will not be a proper dimension.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: Setting an array element with a sequence.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: The requested array has an inhomogeneous shape after one dimension.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: That means the detected shape here is 4 plus in this particular row which is inhomogeneous.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: That means a proper rectangle cannot be made here of 2D array.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So it is not possible to make an array like this.

chunks.title: Creating
chunks.text: So this is the first way of creating NumPy arrays.

text: Next we are going to talk about how do we create NumPy arrays plus we will also discuss a lot of different properties which are associated with NumPy arrays. To create NumPy arrays, let us remove these cells. I will also give you the entire code and the short notes for this entire lecture. So you don't need to worry about me deleting anything. We are actually going to get everything. But it is important that we also code together so that we are also learning things. So the easiest way to create NumPy arrays is to create them from lists. Python has lists and we can directly create them from them. For example, we can write np.array and we can pass any list here. This is a one-dimensional list, a flat list. So we can create our NumPy array from this. We can print the array and we can also print the type of the array. So we have created an array whose type is numpy.ndarray. Similarly, if we want, we can create a separate array. Let's call it array2. We can also print array2 and what we will do in it is we will add a string in it. So let's run the code. So now we have an array of strings. We talked about NumPy arrays being homogeneous. If we send different types of data in the form of a list, then NumPy will try to convert all the data into a single data type. So that is why it is all converted into a single data type. To print the data type, we have a special property which is called array.dtype. We can print the data type from array.dtype and here for this particular array, the data type is U21. Now this is a little strange data type. We will feel that what is the meaning of U here? U means that strings are stored in Unicode format in our Numpy array. Here also, if we want, we can print the data type of the array and this is going to be int64. Meaning integer values are stored in this array. Plus we also have one more property which is the shape property. Shape means dimensions. We can print the shape of these arrays here. Now here because both of these arrays are one dimensional, one dimensional array means a single list like a normal list. So in 1D array, the dimension means the total number of elements. So here we have 5 elements, so the dimension is going to be 5. Here we have 6 elements, so the dimension is going to be 6. Along with this, if we want, we can also create 2D arrays from 2D list. What does 2D list mean? Let's suppose we have some matrix type data like this. We have some rows and we also have some columns. So in this data, if we have data like this, when we talk horizontally, we say that these are our rows. And when we talk vertically, we say that these are our columns. And we can represent this type in the form of 2D list. Let's suppose we have data in our list. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. We will create this data in 2D list first and then convert it in 2D array. So first of all, for 2D list, we actually store the data in terms of all the rows. So in our 2D list, first row will come, second row will come, then third row will come. So in our 2D list, we will have data 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9. And we will convert this data in NumPy array to create a 2D array. We can give it a name here, array2D. And we can print the array 2D. So our 2D array will be created in this way. The same type of matrix has been created which we visualized here. Plus we can also print the shape of this 2D array. Now here we will get a very interesting shape which is 3 x 3. 3 x 3 is basically the array dimension. Whenever we talk about NumPy arrays, when we are printing the shape, shape basically stands for dimension. Dimension means what is the m x n value. What is the row multiplied by column value. Now here we are not multiplying both of them, but we separate them from comma that how many rows and columns we have. In this 2D array, we have 3 rows and 3 columns. That is why the shape is 3 x 3 or 3, 3. If we add one more row in it, 10, 11, and 12. Then our dimension will be 4 x 3. That means we have 4 rows and 3 columns. Our dimension should be a proper rectangular dimension. It is not that we can add numbers randomly. It is not that I can add a 13 after 12. Because this will not be a proper dimension. Setting an array element with a sequence. The requested array has an inhomogeneous shape after one dimension. That means the detected shape here is 4 plus in this particular row which is inhomogeneous. That means a proper rectangle cannot be made here of 2D array. So it is not possible to make an array like this. So this is the first way of creating NumPy arrays.
